elements.sent-2411-001: We develop rigorous approximation and near optimality results for the optimal control of a system which is connected to a controller over a finite rate noiseless channel.
elements.sent-2411-002: While structural results on the optimal encoding and control have been obtained in the literature, their implementation has been prohibitive in general, except for linear models.
elements.sent-2411-003: We develop regularity and structural properties, followed by approximations and reinforcement learning results.
elements.sent-2411-004: Notably, we establish near optimality of finite model approximations as well as sliding finite window coding policies and their reinforcement learning convergence to near optimality.
elements.sent-2411-005: Networked control systems involve stochastic control systems where a communication channel is present between different stations, such as sensors, actuators and controllers.
elements.sent-2411-006: In this context there exists a comprehensive literature on stabilization and optimization of such systems under various information constraints, see e.g. \cite{franceschetti2014elements,Hespanha,MatveevSavkin,YukselBasarBook24,kawan2013invariance for extensive reviews.}
elements.sent-2411-007: Networked control theory requires interdisciplinary methods to achieve optimality and structural results on coders, decoders and controllers.
elements.sent-2411-008: While analytically and architecturally very useful, the structural results on optimal policies often lead to optimization problems in uncountable spaces and hence lead to computationally challenging design problems.
elements.sent-2411-009: Furthermore, despite the presence of such structural results in the literature as we will review, learning theoretic and rigorously justified near optimality results have been very limited.
elements.sent-2411-010: The goal of this paper is to present rigorous reinforcement learning algorithms which are provably near optimal for the optimal coding and control policies in a system which is connected to a controller over a finite rate noiseless channel.
elements.sent-2411-011: We consider a networked control problem where a controlled Markov source observed over a noiseless communication channel is controlled using data obtained from this channel.
elements.sent-2411-012: The Markov source is updated at each time step at a plant with the following dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-013: For $t\geq0,$ \begin{equation}\label{system} x_{t+1} = f(x_t,u_t,w_t),\end{equation} where $x_t$ is defined on finite state space $\X$, $u_t$ is defined on finite action space $\U$, and $w_t$ is some independent and identically distributed noise process, where $x_0$ is a random variable with distribution $\pi_0$.
elements.sent-2411-014: At each time stage $t$, $x_t$ is encoded causally over a noiseless channel.
elements.sent-2411-015: The controller receives information from the communication channel and chooses a control action $u_t$ which is then transmitted to the plant.
elements.sent-2411-016: The encoder and controller use coding and control policies, respectively, to compute their outputs.
elements.sent-2411-017: This system is depicted in Figure \ref{sys .}
elements.figure-2411-001: \label{sys} \begin{center} \includegraphics[height=3.5cm,width=8.5cm]{loop.jpeg} \caption{Control driven Markov process over Noiseless channel \label{loop}} \end{center}
elements.sent-2411-018: A coding policy $\gamma^{e = \{\gamma^e_t, t \ge 0\}$ is a sequence of functions which generate quantization outputs, $q_t$, as a measurable function of the encoder's information at time $t$: \begin{equation}I_t^e = \{{x_{[0,t]},q_{[0,t-1]}}\}, \quad \gamma^e_t:I_t^e\mapsto \M\end{equation} where $\M$ is a finite quantization output alphabet.}
elements.sent-2411-019: A control policy $\gamma^c = \{\gamma^c_t,t \ge 0\}$ is a sequence of functions which generate control actions, $u_t$, as a measurable function of the information available at the controller at time $t$, $I_t^c$: \begin{equation}I_t^c = \{{q_{[0,t]}, u_{[0,t-1]}}\}, \quad \gamma^c_t:I_t^c \mapsto \U\end{equation} where $\U$ is the finite action space.
elements.sent-2411-020: We define the class of admissible joint coding-control policies, $\Gamma_A$, where $\overline{\gamma _t \coloneqq (\gamma^e_t,\gamma^c_t)$, as: \begin{equation}\Gamma_A \coloneqq \{\overline{\gamma} = \{\overline{\gamma}_t, t\geq0\} \}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-021: The solution of this networked control problem involves a joint optimization of coding and control policies.
elements.sent-2411-022: The optimization objective for a finite time horizon is defined as follows for some $N \ge 0$ (see e.g., \cite{yuksel_stochastic_2023 and \cite{yuksel_optimal_2013 ), \begin{equation}\label{objective} J^N(\pi_0) = \inf_{\overline\gamma\in\Gamma_A} J^N(\pi_0,\overline{\gamma}) \coloneqq \inf_{\overline{\gamma}\in\Gamma_A} E_{\pi_0}^{\overline{\gamma}}\left[\sum^{N-1}_{k=0} c(x_k,u_k)\right].\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-023: Here we use $E^{\overline{\gamma _{\pi_0}$ to denote the expectation on $(x_t, u_t)_{t \ge 0}$ given initial distribution $\pi_0$ and joint coding-control policy $\overline{\gamma}$.}}

elements.sent-2411-192: We addressed the problem of optimal control over a finite-rate noiseless communication channel, proving structural results that characterize optimal coding and control policies for a Markovian system.
elements.sent-2411-193: For the purpose of Q-learning, we introduced two methods to approximate this MDP with rigorous convergence and performance guarantees: the finite sliding window and the quantized state space approaches.
elements.sent-2411-194: While the sliding window approach is more practical for real-time systems due to its lower complexity, the quantized state space method allows for more general models and finer approximations but at a higher computational cost.
elements.sent-2411-195: We study the problem of joint optimization involving coding and control policies for a controlled Markovian sytem over a finite-rate noiseless communication channel.
elements.sent-2411-196: We develop regularity and existence results on optimal policies.
elements.sent-2411-197: We then obtain rigorous approximation and near optimality results for jointly optimal coding and control policies.
elements.sent-2411-198: To this end, we first develop existence, regularity, and structural properties on optimal policies, followed by rigorous approximations and reinforcement learning results.
elements.sent-2411-199: Notably, we establish near optimality of finite model approximations obtained via predictor quantization as well as sliding finite window approximations, and their reinforcement learning convergence to near optimality.
elements.sent-2411-200: A detailed comparison of the approximation schemes and their reinforcement learning performance is presented.
elements.sent-2411-201: Networked control theory requires interdisciplinary methods to arrive at optimality and structural results on optimal coders, decoders and controllers.
elements.sent-2411-202: While analytically and architecturally very useful, the structural results on optimal policies often lead to optimization problems in uncountable spaces and hence lead to computationally challenging problems.
elements.sent-2411-203: The goal of this paper is to present rigorous reinforcement learning algorithms which are provably near optimal for the jointly optimal coding and control policies in a system which is connected to a controller over a finite rate noiseless channel.
elements.sent-2411-204: The controlled Markov source is updated at each time step with the following dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-205: For $t\geq0,$ \begin{equation}\label{system} x_{t+1} = f(x_t,u_t,w_t),\end{equation} where $x_t$ takes values from a finite state space $\X$\footnote{The case with continuous spaces will be discussed in Section \ref{contSpaceSec , $u_t$ takes values from a finite action space $\U$, and $w_t$ is some independent and identically distributed (i.i.d.) noise process.}}

elements.sent-2411-208: A coding policy $\gamma^{e = \{\gamma^e_t, t \ge 0\}$ is a sequence of functions which generate quantization outputs, $q_t$, as a measurable function of the encoder's information at time $t$: \begin{equation}I_t^e = \{{x_{[0,t]},q_{[0,t-1]}}\}\end{equation} \begin{equation}\gamma^e_t:I_t^e\mapsto q_t \in \M\end{equation} where $\M$ is a finite quantization output alphabet, so that $q_t = \gamma^e_t(I_t^e)$ for $t \in \mathbb{Z _+$.}}
elements.sent-2411-209: A control policy $\gamma^c = \{\gamma^c_t,t \ge 0\}$ is a sequence of functions which generate control actions, $u_t$, as a measurable function of the information available at the controller at time $t$: \begin{equation}I_t^c = \{{q_{[0,t]}, u_{[0,t-1]}}\}\end{equation} \begin{equation}\gamma^c_t:I_t^c \mapsto u_t \in \U\end{equation} where $\U$ is the finite action space, so that $u_t = \gamma^c_t(I_t^c)$ for all $t \in \mathbb{Z _+$.}
elements.sent-2411-210: With the coding and control policies as given above, we define the class of admissible joint coding-control policies as \begin{equation}\Gamma_A \coloneqq \{\overline{\gamma} = \{(\gamma^e_t,\gamma^c_t), t\geq0\} \}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-211: The networked control problem involves a joint optimization of coding and control policies.
elements.sent-2411-212: The optimization objective for a finite time horizon is defined as follows for some $N \ge 0$: \begin{equation}\label{objective} J^N(\pi_0) = \inf_{\overline\gamma\in\Gamma_A} J^N(\pi_0,\overline{\gamma}) \coloneqq \inf_{\overline{\gamma}\in\Gamma_A} E_{\pi_0}^{\overline{\gamma}}\left[\sum^{N-1}_{k=0} c(x_k,u_k)\right].\end{equation}.

elements.section-2411-009: Comparison of Finite Predictor State and Finite Window Approximations
elements.sent-2411-417: We will now compare the two rigorously justified approximation methods presented: The finite sliding window method and quantized predictor approach.
elements.sent-2411-418: However, the number of possible finite windows grows very fast in the relevant alphabet sizes.
elements.sent-2411-419: Because of this, each iteration requires less computation but, the number of Q-iterations before the algorithm converges to an optimal policy can be very large for window sizes larger than 2.
elements.sent-2411-420: Conversely, the computational complexity at each Q-iteration in the implementation of the quantized predictor approach is significantly higher.
elements.sent-2411-421: Learning requires frequent updates of the belief state (and requires a Bayesian update which necessitates access to the system model), and as the quantization becomes finer, the number of possible states grows exponentially (although not every state may be relevant, as we only need consider those with positive measure under the unique invariant measure, see Theorem \ref{theorem:quantizedQlearning_nearoptimal ).}
elements.sent-2411-422: Thus, even for resolutions of quantization up to 15, the number of visited states is small enough that the algorithm can converge relatively faster than the sliding window method (for window sizes greater than 2).
elements.sent-2411-423: This leads to a trade-off between approximation accuracy and computational costs for both these methods.
elements.sent-2411-424: Provided the window length is sufficiently large, it converges to near-optimal policies regardless of the initial distribution, making it advantageous in real-world applications where the initial state might not be precisely known or controllable.
elements.sent-2411-425: The quantized predictor state space method is sensitive to initialization.
elements.sent-2411-426: The learned policies from this approach are near-optimal only when the initial state has positive measure under the unique invariant measure for $\pi_t$.
elements.sent-2411-427: This may be problematic in practical scenarios where the system may start from an arbitrary initial condition.
elements.sent-2411-428: Only under appropriate conditions, such as those characterized by the Dobrushin coefficient, can this approach have near-optimal performance.
elements.sent-2411-429: The quantized state space method can work under weaker conditions of stability, and does not require an assumption on the Dobrushin coefficient.
elements.sent-2411-430: The quantized state space method, while computationally less efficient, offers flexibility and can be more accurate in scenarios where the source’s initial distribution is well-known and the computational resources to handle the Bayesian updates are available.

elements.sent-2411-480: This work has addressed the problem of optimal control over a finite-rate noiseless communication channel, proving structural results that characterize optimal coding and control policies for a Markovian system.
elements.sent-2411-481: For the purpose of Q-learning, we introduced two methods to approximate this MDP: the finite sliding window and the quantized state space approaches.
elements.sent-2411-482: We demonstrated the effectiveness of Q-learning for both methods and noted the trade-offs between computational efficiency and initialization sensitivity.
elements.sent-2411-483: While the sliding window approach is more practical for real-time systems due to its lower complexity, the quantized state space method allows for finer approximations but at a higher computational cost.
elements.sent-2411-484: Future research could extend these methods to more complex scenarios, such as noisy channels, channels with feedback, and systems with infinite state and action spaces.
